---
title: "OCI Wazuh Deployment"
date: "07-20-2026"
categories: [guides]
format: 
  html:
    code-fold: true
    code-summary: "Show the code"
    code-block-background: true
execute:
  freeze: true
---

![Wazuh and OCI](./images/Wazuh-OCI.png)

# Introduction

The following post aims to assist in the deployment of [Wazuh](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/getting-started/index.html) as a centralized Security Information and Event Magament (SIEM) for Oracle Cloud Infrastructure (OCI) via a private Oracle Kubernetes Engine (OKE) cluster. Additionally, we will discuss on how to enroll compute instances and import OCI logs into the SIEM.

Through this blog, we will have accomplished the following tasks:

* Created a private OKE Cluster
* Configured VCN, Subnets, and Security Lists
* Prepared OCI Block Storage
* Updated Wazuh Kubernetes manifest for OCI
* Deployed and Validated Wazuh 

## What is Wauzh?


As stated before, [Wazuh](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/getting-started/index.html) is a SIEM solution, and is comprised of four main components:

* [Wazuh Indexer](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/installation-guide/wazuh-indexer/index.html) stores data and alerts which the Wazuh Server creates. 
* [Wazuh Server](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/installation-guide/wazuh-server/index.html) serves as the analytics engine which detect threats or anomalies that are collected from endpoints. 
* [Wazuh Dashboard](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/installation-guide/wazuh-dashboard/index.html) visualizes alert and data from the Wazuh Server and Indexer, and highlights where agents are deployed.
* [Wazuh Agent(s)](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/installation-guide/wazuh-agent/index.html) deploys on endpoints to forward collected data to the Wazuh Server.

Further, all four components must be running together, otherwise Wazuh will not function correctly. 

For further visualization on how these four components work together, refer to the following diagram from Wazuh.

![Wazuh Architecture](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/_images/deployment-architecture1.png)


In essence, the agents forwards data from endpoints to the server which generates alerts for the indexer to store, and all of this information can be viewed ffom the dashboard.


# Deployment

Wazuh has multiple deployment models which can be found on its [installation page](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/installation-guide/index.html) and [alternative installations page](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/deployment-options/index.html).

For this blog, we will focus on the [Deployment on Kubernetes](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/deployment-options/deploying-with-kubernetes/index.html) section. 

## Deploy with OKE



### Configuring OKE Cluster

OKE allows for two creation methods for clusters which are Quick Create or Custom Create. For this blog, we will be using Quick Create as it provisions the Virtual Cloud Network (VCN), Internet Gateway (IG), NAT Gateway (NAT), Service Gateway (SGW), Cluster, and Node Pool for us.

When creating the OKE cluster through Quick Creation, we will expect similar values for the following:

| Component | New value |
|---|---|
| Cluster | `AB-Quick-Cluster` |
| Cluster type | Basic |
| Kubernetes | `v1.36.1` |
| Network type | OCI VCN-native |
| VCN | Quick Create-generated VCN |
| VCN CIDR | `10.0.0.0/16` |
| Private API subnet | `10.0.0.0/28` |
| Private API endpoint | `10.0.0.11:6443` |
| Worker-node subnet | `10.0.10.0/24`  |
| Service LB subnet | `10.0.20.0/24` |
| Kubernetes Service CIDR | `10.96.0.0/16` |
| Network Security Groups | None |
| Security Lists | Three Quick Create-generated lists |


Do note that our networking configuration may differ, so do take note of your given values. Additionally, you may add another subnet that allows for public addressing, and a separate security list for access. For this blog, the following subnet and security list has been created in the OKE VCN for Wazuh Acess via a Compute Instance through a local forwarding SSH tunnel.

| Component | New Value |
|---|---|
| Compute Subnet | `10.0.30.0/24`|
| Security List | OKE-Wazuh-Compute |
| Security List Ingress | SSH from 0.0.0.0/0 |

These are just example values, so please ensure the values match your environment.

 

### Configuring Wazuh for the Cluster

There are multiple methods on accessing the private OKE cluster to configure Wazuh which includes OCI Bastion Service, OCI Shell, or via a Compute Instance. For now, we will be configuring Wazuh via a Compute Instance is on the same VCN as the OKE cluster and can access its nodes. Moreover, the instance must be publically accessible via SSH at the minimum.

For now, I have created a simple Oracle Linux 9 Instance that it on the `10.0.30.0/24` subnet.



 


# Leveraging Agents


# OCI Logs into Wazuh


# Summary
